_metadata.notes: Each figure shows a cross-section at a specific medullary level, from caudal (pyramidal decussation) to rostral (pontomedullary junction). The medulla is divided into: basis (pyramid), tegmentum (everything dorsal to pyramid), and tectum (4th ventricle roof structures). p-12 is a summary figure showing arterial territories and clinical syndrome zones across multiple levels.

anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the level of the pyramidal (motor) decussation. The corticospinal fibers are crossing from the ventromedial pyramid to the contralateral lateral funiculus. Nucleus gracilis and nucleus cuneatus are prominent dorsally. The spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus are visible laterally. This is the cervicomedullary junction zone.

tracts.zone: ventromedial medulla, decussating fibers crossing midline
tracts.somatotopy: upper limb fibers decussate more rostrally, lower limb fibers more caudally
tracts.notes: The decussation of pyramids. Arm fibers cross rostral to leg fibers. A cruciate paralysis pattern (arms > legs or legs > arms) results from partial lesions here.
Second-order neuron for fasciculus gracilis. Located just deep to the dorsal surface at the midline.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral medulla, lateral to nucleus gracilis
tracts.notes: Second-order neuron for fasciculus cuneatus. Located lateral to nucleus gracilis.

tracts.somatotopy: ipsilateral face pain/temperature (onion-skin distribution: perioral = rostral pars oralis, peripheral = caudal pars caudalis)
tracts.notes: The tract is on the surface; the nucleus (pars caudalis at this level) is deep to it. Continuation of Lissauer's tract equivalent for trigeminal system.

tracts.zone: lateral medulla, deep to spinal trigeminal tract
tracts.notes: Resembles the substantia gelatinosa of the spinal cord. Pars caudalis extends from obex down to C2-C3. Important in Wallenberg syndrome.
Below the level of decussation, some fibers are already in the lateral funiculus.

tracts.somatotopy: sacral fibers lateral, cervical fibers medial
tracts.notes: Continuous from spinal cord. Lies on the lateral/anterolateral surface of the medulla at this level.

tracts.zone: lateral surface, ventral to spinal trigeminal tract
tracts.notes: Continues rostrally on the lateral surface. Will enter cerebellum via superior cerebellar peduncle at pons level.

tracts.zone: lateral surface, dorsal portion, joining inferior cerebellar peduncle
tracts.notes: At this level begins to aggregate with other fibers forming the inferior cerebellar peduncle (restiform body).
Contains autonomic and respiratory neurons. Continuous with spinal cord central gray.
Descending from hypothalamus through lateral brainstem tegmentum. Damage here causes ipsilateral Horner syndrome. Located in the PICA vascular territory.
Equivalent of Clarke's nucleus for upper limb. Relays proprioception to cerebellum via inferior cerebellar peduncle. Only present at cervicomedullary junction and caudal medulla levels.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Caudal Medulla - Sensory Decussation (Internal Arcuate Fibers)
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the level of the sensory decussation. Internal arcuate fibers from nucleus gracilis and cuneatus sweep ventromedially, cross the midline, and form the medial lemniscus. The pyramid is now fully formed ventrally. The hypoglossal nucleus appears dorsally near the floor of the 4th ventricle. The dorsal motor nucleus of vagus is visible. Inferior olivary nucleus is beginning to appear.

tracts.zone: central medulla, sweeping from dorsolateral to ventromedial, crossing midline
tracts.somatotopy: gracile (leg) and cuneate (arm) fibers crossing together
tracts.notes: The great sensory decussation. After crossing, fibers form the medial lemniscus. A midline lesion here causes bilateral proprioception/vibration loss.

tracts.zone: paramedian, dorsal to pyramid, vertical orientation
tracts.somatotopy: at this level: leg ventral, arm dorsal (will rotate as it ascends)
tracts.notes: Just formed after sensory decussation. Oriented vertically (parasagittal) at this level. In ASA / medial medullary territory.

tracts.somatotopy: face lateral, arm intermediate, leg medial (at medullary pyramid level)
tracts.notes: The fully formed pyramid. Contains corticospinal and corticobulbar fibers. Visible as the prominent ventral bulge. Supplied by vertebral artery / ASA branches.

tracts.zone: dorsomedial tegmentum, floor of 4th ventricle, paramedian
tracts.notes: Located in the hypoglossal trigone on the floor of the 4th ventricle. A medial structure — involved in medial medullary (Dejerine) syndrome when CN XII root is affected.

tracts.zone: traversing ventromedially from hypoglossal nucleus, exiting between pyramid and olive
tracts.notes: Root fibers pass ventromedially through the tegmentum, past the medial lemniscus, to exit between pyramid and olive. Damage here causes ipsilateral tongue weakness with deviation toward the lesion.

tracts.tract_name: Dorsal motor nucleus of vagus (CN X)
tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, lateral to hypoglossal nucleus
tracts.notes: Parasympathetic motor nucleus. Supplies thoracic and abdominal viscera via vagus nerve. Located in the vagal trigone of the 4th ventricle floor.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, lateral to dorsal motor nucleus of vagus
tracts.somatotopy: rostral: taste (CN VII, IX); caudal: visceral sensation (CN IX, X)
tracts.notes: The tract surrounds the nucleus. At this caudal level, primarily involved in visceral sensation (cardiorespiratory reflexes). In PICA territory.

tracts.zone: lateral medulla, surface layer (tract) with nucleus deep to it
tracts.notes: Still at pars caudalis level. The tract is a surface landmark on the lateral medulla.

tracts.zone: anterolateral medulla, between olive and spinal trigeminal tract
tracts.notes: Located in the lateral medulla. In the territory of PICA/vertebral artery. Affected in lateral medullary syndrome.
Contains posterior spinocerebellar tract, cuneocerebellar fibers, olivocerebellar fibers (climbing fibers). Building up at this level, will become prominent more rostrally.

tracts.zone: dorsomedial tegmentum, paramedian, ventral to 4th ventricle floor
tracts.notes: Located adjacent to the midline, just ventral to the floor of the 4th ventricle. Carries vestibular projections to oculomotor nuclei and descending fibers for head/neck coordination.

tracts.zone: central tegmentum, between olive and 4th ventricle floor
tracts.notes: Occupies the core of the tegmentum. Contains respiratory, cardiovascular, and arousal centers.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, near spinal trigeminal complex
tracts.notes: Runs through lateral tegmentum. In PICA territory. Damage causes Horner syndrome (Wallenberg).

The inferior olive is beginning to appear at this level. It will become the dominant landmark of the mid-medulla. Relays motor information to cerebellum via climbing fibers through inferior cerebellar peduncle.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Mid-Medulla - Inferior Olivary Nucleus and Hypoglossal Nucleus
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the mid-medulla level. The inferior olivary nucleus is now a prominent folded (corrugated) structure in the ventrolateral tegmentum. The medial lemniscus is a well-defined vertical band paramedian, dorsal to the pyramid. The hypoglossal nucleus and nerve root are visible. The dorsal motor nucleus of vagus and the nucleus ambiguus are present. The 4th ventricle is open dorsally.

tracts.notes: Well-defined at this level. Bordered laterally by the inferior olive. ASA/vertebral territory.

tracts.zone: paramedian, dorsal to pyramid, oriented vertically
tracts.notes: Vertically oriented parasagittal band between pyramid and tegmentum. In the medial medullary territory (ASA/vertebral). Key structure in Dejerine syndrome.

Prominent landmark of the mid-medulla. Olivocerebellar fibers cross midline and enter inferior cerebellar peduncle. The hilum faces medially. Palatal myoclonus results from lesions in the Guillain-Mollaret triangle (dentate-red nucleus-inferior olive).

tracts.zone: dorsomedial tegmentum, paramedian, floor of 4th ventricle
tracts.notes: In the hypoglossal trigone. Medial structure. Its root fibers traverse ventromedially between the olive and the pyramid.

tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, lateral to hypoglossal nucleus, vagal trigone
tracts.notes: Parasympathetic preganglionic neurons for viscera. Located in the vagal trigone of 4th ventricle floor.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, deep within reticular formation
tracts.notes: Motor nucleus for CN IX, X, and cranial root of XI. Supplies pharynx, larynx, and upper esophagus. Located deep in the lateral reticular formation. In PICA territory. Damage causes dysphagia and hoarseness (Wallenberg syndrome).
At this level, both taste and visceral sensory components are present. In PICA territory.

tracts.tract_name: Spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus (pars interpolaris)
tracts.somatotopy: ipsilateral face pain/temperature and dental pain
tracts.notes: At this level, the nucleus transitions from pars caudalis to pars interpolaris. Pars interpolaris is particularly important for dental pain referral patterns.

tracts.zone: lateral medulla, between inferior olive and spinal trigeminal tract
tracts.notes: In the lateral medulla. Overlapping territory of PICA and vertebral artery. Affected in Wallenberg syndrome.
Now a well-formed bundle containing posterior spinocerebellar, cuneocerebellar, and olivocerebellar fibers. Damage causes ipsilateral cerebellar ataxia (Wallenberg). PICA territory.

tracts.zone: dorsomedial tegmentum, paramedian, ventral to 4th ventricle floor
tracts.notes: Paramedian location near 4th ventricle. Carries descending vestibulospinal connections and ascending vestibulo-oculomotor connections.

tracts.zone: central tegmentum, large area between olive and 4th ventricle floor
tracts.notes: Extensive at this level. Contains lateral and medial reticular nuclei. Involved in respiratory rhythm generation, cardiovascular regulation, swallowing.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, dorsolateral to inferior olive
tracts.notes: In the lateral tegmentum, near nucleus ambiguus and spinal trigeminal complex. PICA territory. Wallenberg syndrome causes Horner.

tracts.zone: medial to the main inferior olivary nucleus
tracts.notes: Part of the olivary complex. Sends climbing fibers to cerebellum.

tracts.zone: dorsal to the main inferior olivary nucleus
tracts.notes: Part of the olivary complex.
Serotonergic neurons in the midline raphe. Part of the descending pain modulation system.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Mid-Rostral Medulla - Vagal Level / Full Inferior Olive
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the level where the inferior olivary nucleus is at its maximum extent. The 4th ventricle is wide open with the choroid plexus laterally. Vagal structures (dorsal motor nucleus, nucleus ambiguus, solitary nucleus) are all prominent. The inferior cerebellar peduncle is large. Cerebellar tissue visible dorsally (vermis and tonsils flanking the 4th ventricle).

tracts.notes: Stable location through the medulla. Flanked by the inferior olive laterally.

tracts.somatotopy: leg ventral, arm dorsal (beginning to rotate)
tracts.notes: Still vertical parasagittal orientation but beginning to rotate toward horizontal as it ascends toward pons.
At maximum size. The hilum opens medially. Olivocerebellar fibers exit the hilum, cross the midline, and enter the contralateral inferior cerebellar peduncle.

tracts.system: motor nucleus (CN IX, X, XI cranial root)
tracts.zone: deep lateral tegmentum, within reticular formation
tracts.somatotopy: pharynx, larynx (CN IX, X), trapezius/SCM (CN XI cranial root)
tracts.notes: Prominent at this level. Supplies motor fibers to pharyngeal and laryngeal muscles. Damage causes dysphagia, hoarseness, absent gag reflex (Wallenberg).

tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, vagal trigone of 4th ventricle floor
tracts.notes: Prominent parasympathetic nucleus. Cardiovascular and GI parasympathetic outflow.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, lateral to dorsal motor nucleus
tracts.somatotopy: visceral sensation, baroreceptor/chemoreceptor input (CN IX, X)
tracts.notes: Major relay for visceral afferents including carotid body (CN IX) and aortic arch (CN X) chemoreceptors and baroreceptors. PICA territory.

tracts.tract_name: Spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus (pars interpolaris to pars oralis transition)
tracts.notes: Transitioning from pars interpolaris to pars oralis at more rostral medullary levels.
PICA territory. Affected in Wallenberg syndrome causing contralateral body pain/temperature loss.
At maximum size before entering the cerebellum. Contains spinocerebellar, cuneocerebellar, olivocerebellar, vestibulocerebellar fibers. PICA territory.
Consistent paramedian dorsomedial position.
Rostral extent of the hypoglossal nucleus. Tapers at this level.
Contains respiratory rhythm generators (pre-Botzinger complex is nearby). Cardiovascular control centers.
In the reticular formation of the lateral tegmentum. PICA territory.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Rostral Medulla - Cochlear and Vestibular Nuclei Level
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the rostral medulla. The cochlear nuclei (dorsal and ventral) wrap around the inferior cerebellar peduncle. The vestibular nuclei complex is prominent in the lateral floor of the 4th ventricle. The inferior olive is beginning to diminish. Stria medullaris (of 4th ventricle) may be visible crossing the floor. The lateral recess of the 4th ventricle is open.

tracts.notes: Continues unchanged at the ventral surface.

tracts.zone: paramedian, dorsal to pyramid, beginning to rotate from vertical toward horizontal
tracts.notes: Beginning the rotation from parasagittal (vertical) to coronal (horizontal) orientation that will be complete at the pons. ASA territory.
Tapering off at the rostral medulla level. Will disappear by the pontomedullary junction.

tracts.zone: lateral floor of 4th ventricle, large area
tracts.notes: Includes medial, lateral (Deiters), superior, and inferior vestibular nuclei. Located in the vestibular area of the 4th ventricle floor. Damage causes vertigo, nystagmus, nausea/vomiting, and gait ataxia. PICA territory. Key component of Wallenberg syndrome.

tracts.zone: dorsal surface of inferior cerebellar peduncle, forming the acoustic tubercle
tracts.notes: The dorsal cochlear nucleus sits on top of the ICP like a cap. Second-order auditory neuron. Stria of Held/Monakow arise from here.

tracts.zone: anterolateral surface of inferior cerebellar peduncle
tracts.notes: Wraps around the ventral aspect of the ICP. CN VIII auditory fibers enter between the two cochlear nuclei. Gives rise to trapezoid body fibers that cross to form the lateral lemniscus.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral medulla, turning to enter cerebellum
tracts.notes: At this level, the ICP is curving dorsally to enter the cerebellum. The cochlear nuclei drape over it.

tracts.tract_name: Spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus (pars oralis)
tracts.somatotopy: ipsilateral face pain/temperature (perioral region in pars oralis)
tracts.notes: At the rostral medulla, we are in pars oralis territory. This part receives perioral sensory input.
Continues in its lateral medullary position. Will transition into the pons.

tracts.tract_name: Solitary tract and nucleus (rostral portion - taste)
tracts.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, medial to vestibular nuclei
tracts.somatotopy: taste from anterior 2/3 tongue (CN VII) and posterior 1/3 (CN IX)
tracts.notes: At this rostral level, the gustatory (taste) function predominates. PICA territory.
Important relay between vestibular nuclei and oculomotor nuclei. Damage causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (more clinically significant at pontine/midbrain levels).
Contains autonomic, respiratory, and emetic centers. Vomiting center (area postrema nearby).
PICA territory. Continues through lateral tegmentum.

anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the pontomedullary junction. The pontine nuclei and transverse pontine fibers are beginning to appear ventrally. The vestibular nuclei remain prominent. The facial nucleus (CN VII) may be appearing. The inferior cerebellar peduncle has largely entered the cerebellum. The lateral lemniscus is forming from cochlear nucleus projections.

tracts.zone: ventromedial, now becoming embedded in pontine base
tracts.notes: The corticospinal fibers transition from the isolated pyramid into the basis pontis where they become interspersed with pontine nuclei and transverse pontine fibers.

tracts.zone: ventral tegmentum, rotating from vertical to horizontal (transverse)
tracts.notes: The medial lemniscus is rotating from its vertical parasagittal orientation in the medulla to horizontal in the pons. At the junction, it is at an intermediate angle.
Lateral (Deiters) and superior vestibular nuclei are prominent at this level. The lateral vestibular nucleus gives rise to the lateral vestibulospinal tract.

tracts.tract_name: Spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus (pars oralis, rostral)
tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, transitioning to principal sensory nucleus territory
tracts.somatotopy: ipsilateral face (perioral emphasis at pars oralis)
tracts.notes: Pars oralis merges with the principal (chief) sensory nucleus of V at the pontine level. The rostral end of the spinal trigeminal system.
Now in the lateral tegmentum of the pontomedullary junction.
Connecting vestibular nuclei to oculomotor nuclei. Also carries tectospinal fibers.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, lateral to spinothalamic tract
tracts.notes: Formed from crossed (trapezoid body) and uncrossed fibers from cochlear nuclei. Ascending to inferior colliculus. Unilateral lesion causes minimal hearing loss due to bilateral representation.
Rostral gustatory portion. Taste fibers from CN VII (chorda tympani) and CN IX synapse here.
Pontine reticular formation begins here. Involved in arousal, eye movement coordination, and motor planning.
Continues in the lateral tegmentum at the pontomedullary junction.
Contains descending fibers from red nucleus to inferior olive (part of Guillain-Mollaret triangle) and ascending fibers. Palatal myoclonus results from lesions.

AICA_PICA_overlap: lateral pontomedullary junction is a watershed zone between PICA and AICA territories

anatomical_levels.level_name: Medulla - Arterial Territory and Clinical Syndrome Overview
anatomical_levels.description: Summary figure showing multiple medullary cross-sections with arterial territory overlays. Demonstrates the vascular supply at different medullary levels and the structures affected in lateral medullary (Wallenberg/PICA) and medial medullary (Dejerine/ASA-vertebral) syndromes. Shows the transition from caudal to rostral medulla with color-coded arterial territories.

structures_affected.deficit: vertigo, nystagmus, nausea/vomiting, falling toward lesion side
ipsilateral vocal cord paralysis (hoarseness), dysphagia, absent gag reflex

structures_spared: Pyramid (corticospinal tract) - spared, no motor weakness
Hypoglossal nucleus/nerve - spared, tongue function normal
lateral_medullary_syndrome_Wallenberg.key_clinical_feature: Crossed sensory pattern: ipsilateral face + contralateral body pain/temperature loss. NO motor weakness (pyramid is in medial territory).

medial_medullary_syndrome_Dejerine.artery: Anterior spinal artery or vertebral artery (paramedian branches)

structures_affected.deficit: contralateral hemiparesis (UMN type, spares face initially)
ipsilateral tongue weakness with deviation toward lesion side

structures_spared: Lateral tegmentum (all PICA territory structures) - spared
Spinal trigeminal - spared, face sensation normal
Inferior cerebellar peduncle - spared, no ataxia
medial_medullary_syndrome_Dejerine.key_clinical_feature: Classic triad: contralateral hemiparesis + contralateral proprioception loss + ipsilateral tongue deviation. Rare syndrome.

posterior_spinal_artery.clinical_syndrome: Rare; dorsal column nuclei dysfunction with proprioceptive loss

AICA_overlap_zone.clinical_syndrome: AICA syndrome features overlap (hearing loss, facial weakness, vertigo)
